subject: Make Controller policy type derived from native
commit_message:
Make Controller policy type derived from native

onap.policies.drools.Controller has been made to
be derived from native, and it has been consolidated
under the onap.policies.native.Drools.yaml.
Additionallly some renaming of fields has been done in
an attempt to be more representative of its content.

Issue-ID: POLICY-2388
Signed-off-by: jhh <jorge.hernandez-herrero@att.com>
Change-Id: I5b1cf04f36db01b917b77eff4ab5254073be9b0c
Signed-off-by: jhh <jorge.hernandez-herrero@att.com>

Patch Set 1:

These are the changes I mentioned in the committer's meeting.  Are you guys ok with these changes?

Patch Set 1:

Does operational policy use this controller policy? I see "native" embedded in this policy type name.

Patch Set 1:

I thought the new operational drools policy type also used onap.policies.drools.Controller

Patch Set 1:

> Does operational policy use this controller policy? I see "native"
 > embedded in this policy type name.

There is no linkage in the tosca definition between the *.drools.Controller one and the operational ones (the *Controller one derives from root same as the operational ones).   I don't see much of a relationship between operational and this controller one, other than the operational may contain a controllerName (which is optional) that may match one create with this policy.  I see the Controller one, as a native one, not operational related, which is more control loop oriented.

Patch Set 1:

> I thought the new operational drools policy type also used
 > onap.policies.drools.Controller

In the spec, I don't see that they are linked.   I see it more a relationship with the "native.Drools" one.   The fact that the "native.Drools" one would not make sense without the "Controller" one, that's the stronger relationship, and the reason to make the controller one native.   The operational ones, don't need any of these 2 "native" policies to operate, and that's the usual case in our software.

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> > I thought the new operational drools policy type also used
>  > onap.policies.drools.Controller
> 
> In the spec, I don't see that they are linked.   I see it more a relationship with the "native.Drools" one.   The fact that the "native.Drools" one would not make sense without the "Controller" one, that's the stronger relationship, and the reason to make the controller one native.   The operational ones, don't need any of these 2 "native" policies to operate, and that's the usual case in our software.

Ok - I think we were thinking there was ONE definition for a controller. And that definition is shared between drools operaitonal and drools native.

Patch Set 1:

> > Patch Set 1:
 > >
 > > > I thought the new operational drools policy type also used
 > >  > onap.policies.drools.Controller
 > >
 > > In the spec, I don't see that they are linked.   I see it more a
 > relationship with the "native.Drools" one.   The fact that the
 > "native.Drools" one would not make sense without the "Controller"
 > one, that's the stronger relationship, and the reason to make the
 > controller one native.   The operational ones, don't need any of
 > these 2 "native" policies to operate, and that's the usual case in
 > our software.
 > 
 > Ok - I think we were thinking there was ONE definition for a
 > controller. And that definition is shared between drools
 > operaitonal and drools native.

I see, it's not defined in the tosca schemas, there is a loose relationship based on an optional field (controllerName) that's the only commonality (but at runtime).  In any case, I really think that Drools and Controller have a strong bound (a dependence relationship, the Drools one makes no sense without the controller one, and the drools ones is plainly a rules artifact).   The relationship between the operational and any of these 2 is very loose, if any, the operational does not need any of these 2 to operate.
